text: This is a thirty-two-year-old female who presents with lower GI bleed and malnutrition secondary to ulcerative colitis, which is refractory to medical management. She is taken to the operating room for a laparoscopic total colectomy. A laparoscopic total colectomy is performed in a medial-lateral fashion.

The surgeon begins dissection of the left colon by identifying the ligament of Treitz. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is located lateral to the ligament, skeletonized, and divided. The left mesocolon is retracted anteriorly, revealing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The ureter is visible crossing the pelvic brim. Dissection continues to the left colic branch of the IMA, with retroperitoneal structures being swept down, and the IMA is divided.

The surgeon dissects the lateral attachments of the rectosigmoid colon and divides the colon at the rectosigmoid junction using an endogia stapler. The mesocolon is separated from the underlying retroperitoneal structures, and the splenic flexure is fully mobilized.

The surgeon identifies, skeletonizes, and divides the left middle colic vessels. Dissection of the right colon begins with identification of the ileocolic pedicle, followed by retraction and dissection. The duodenum is visualized, and retroperitoneal structures are swept down. The ileocolic vessels are divided, and the right middle colic vessels are identified, skeletonized, and divided.
The surgeon takes down the hepatic flexure, followed by the dissection of the lateral attachments of the right colon from the abdominal wall. The distal ileum is then divided.

A dilator is inserted into the rectum, followed by the use of electrocautery to open the rectal stump. Ring forceps are introduced transanally into the abdomen to grasp the specimen, which is then extracted through the anus under direct visualization. The rectal stump is stapled off, and the remnant rectum is removed from the abdomen.
